nativeRange.habitat: The Curly Cue Anemone is found across shallow Caribbean reefs, seagrass beds, and sand pockets among Halimeda algae, typically from depths of 1 to 40 metres. It anchors the base of its column (the pedal disc) deep into rock crevices, holes, or beneath ledges, leaving only the tentacle crown exposed above the substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with deep crevices for pedal disc anchoring
protected overhangs out of direct flow
ample buffer space around other sessile inverts

diet.notes: Target-feed meaty marine foods 2–3 times per week. Suitable foods include small chopped pieces of fish, mussels, shrimp, krill, and other crustacean flesh, plus occasional zooplankton or phytoplankton. A baster or feeding stick placed gently onto the tentacles works well. Nutrition is supplemented by photosynthetic zooxanthellae, so good lighting reduces feeding pressure but does not replace target feeding.

goodWith: Red snapping pistol shrimp (Alpheus armatus) — natural symbiont
larger passive reef fish too big to be stung
avoidWith: small fish that may be stung and eaten
ornamental shrimp (other than the symbionts above)
stony and soft corals placed within tentacle reach
other anemone species in close proximity
Indo-Pacific clownfish (do not host this species)
starfish, nudibranchs, and sea spiders (documented predators)

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield; 1024x768; in-situ whole anemone showing the complete tentacle crown with the diagnostic white spiral (ringed) banding; sharpest complete specimen found.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720; crisp macro that clearly resolves the characteristic white corkscrew whorls on the translucent tentacles; feature close-up so not the lead image.
Jay Brasher; 1024x768; ringed tentacles with a Pederson cleaner shrimp, illustrating the species' natural symbiosis; slightly dark/soft so kept as a supporting shot.

summary: The Curly Cue Anemone (Bartholomea annulata) is a Caribbean anemone with long translucent tentacles ringed by white spiral whorls. Often sold as a hardy non-pest alternative to Aiptasia, it is best known for its natural mutualism with the red snapping pistol shrimp Alpheus armatus.
careNotes:
Bartholomea annulata is a photosynthetic carnivore native to the Caribbean Sea, Gulf of Mexico, and Western Atlantic, ranging from Texas and Florida south to the northern coast of South America. It belongs to family Aiptasiidae alongside the pest glass anemones (Aiptasia), but is a desirable hobby species in its own right — distinct in size, appearance, and its ringed corkscrew tentacles.

Key identification: a small column (often hidden in a rock crevice or sand pocket) bearing roughly 200 long, translucent tentacles marked with characteristic white spiral bands, giving the species its common names "Curly Cue" and "Corkscrew". A faint nighttime fluorescence has been reported under actinic light.

IMPORTANT COMPATIBILITY NOTE: Despite being sold in the reef trade, B. annulata is a Caribbean species and does NOT naturally host clownfish — that symbiosis evolved exclusively in Indo-Pacific Stichodactyla/Heteractis/Entacmaea anemones. Pair it instead with its true natural symbiont, the Red Snapping Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus armatus) and/or Caribbean cleaner shrimp (Ancylomenes pedersoni, Periclimenes yucatanicus). These pairings produce some of the most authentic Caribbean biotope displays achievable in a home reef.

Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate to strong reef illumination to support zooxanthellae (the photosynthetic algae living inside the anemone's tissues). As a generally shallow-reef species, it tolerates a wide range; a PAR of around 75–200 is a reasonable working band, with lower placements acceptable for specimens from deeper water.
• Water flow: Moderate, indirect flow. Strong direct flow tears the long tentacles, and the anemone may relocate to escape it. Avoid placing near powerheads or overflows — the long tentacles tangle easily.
• Placement: Allow it to choose its own spot, ideally a rock crevice or sand pocket where the column can hide and only tentacles are exposed. Provide several centimeters of sand and a rocky overhang. Once settled, it tends to stay put for long periods.
• Tentacle reach and stinging: Tentacles can extend 12 cm or more, and the total stinging footprint of a mature anemone can be 25–30 cm. Keep it well clear of all corals and other sessile invertebrates.
• Water chemistry: Stable reef parameters are required. Supplementing calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace elements is broadly advised, as for any photosynthetic invertebrate; regular partial water changes are strongly recommended.
• Tank maturity: Generally advised to add to a well-established system (3 months or more) with stable water chemistry.
• Reproduction in captivity: Has been documented reproducing asexually via pedal laceration — small fragments left behind on rocks regenerate into new anemones.

Although the anemone itself is not actively aggressive, its long stinging tentacles will damage neighbouring invertebrates and can capture small fish or shrimp, making a minimum tank of around 150 L advisable to allow adequate buffer space around other inhabitants. If keeping the natural Alpheus armatus pistol shrimp symbiosis, the extra volume also helps accommodate a shrimp burrow system.
breedingNotes: The Curly Cue Anemone reproduces both sexually and asexually. In the wild, it spawns by releasing eggs and sperm into the water column, where fertilization takes place externally and produces free-swimming planktonic larvae; a biannual reproductive cycle has been documented. In captivity, the practical pathway is asexual reproduction via pedal laceration — small fragments of the basal disc are left behind when the anemone relocates, and these regenerate into new individuals. Deliberate sexual breeding under aquarium conditions is rarely attempted.

primary.name: Reeflings – Bartholomea annulata Care Guide
primary.notes: LiveAquaria product page (the manifest's designated primary) returned HTTP 404, so Reeflings was promoted to primary. Provided taxonomy (Aiptasiidae, Actiniaria), depth (1–40 m), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026 SG), minimum tank (100 L), reef-safe yes, peaceful temperament, carnivore diet, asexual reproduction via pedal laceration, and Pederson/red snapping shrimp symbiosis.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Curly-Cue Anemone Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), specific gravity (1.023–1.025), care level (moderate), aggressive temperament rating (averaged with Reeflings' peaceful to semi-aggressive), minimum tank (55 gal / 208 L), reef-compatible yes, carnivore diet of fish/mussels/crustaceans, and preference for protected crevices out of direct flow. Family Aiptasiidae confirmed.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Bartholomea annulata
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy, full native range (Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, from Bermuda/Florida/Texas south to northern South America), depth (~40 m), morphology (oral disc ~12 cm, ~200 ringed tentacles, ~30 cm extended diameter), symbionts (Ancylomenes pedersoni, Periclimenes yucatanicus, Heteromysis actiniae), and predators (starfish, nudibranchs, sea spiders including Pigrogromitus timsanus).

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web – Bartholomea annulata
additional.notes: Confirmed Alpheus armatus pistol shrimp mutualism (the natural symbiont that should be paired with this anemone in a Caribbean biotope setup), Periclimenes yucatanicus association, biannual reproductive cycle, depth range (1–40 m), and oviparous external fertilization.
